ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for stir-fried baby clams with rice cracker; closest parent 'clam food product' (FOODON_03302757) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW (Clams and cockles, cooked) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code exists for stir-fried baby clams with rice cracker.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of stir-fried baby clams with rice cracker is approximately 300 g, comprising a generous portion of clams tossed with aromatics and accompanied by crispy rice crackers.

nutrient_source.title: Clams, mixed species, cooked, moist heat (USDA SR Legacy, NDB 15159) combined with Rice crackers (USDA SR Legacy, NDB 19016) in estimated dish proportion (approx. 60% clams, 25% rice cracker, 15% aromatics/oil)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for this dish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values estimated by weighted combination: 60% cooked clams (USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 175177), 25% rice crackers (USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 175025), 15% aromatics and cooking oil (garlic, onion, ginger, chilli, vegetable oil). Energy and macros cross-checked against FAO/INFOODS SEA shellfish dish analogues. Values represent a reasonable per-100g estimate for the combined dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice crackers / rice cakes analogous estimate; clam component contributes negligible GI
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this dish. The dominant glycemic contributor is the rice cracker (banh da). Rice crackers have a reported GI of approximately 82 (University of Sydney GI Database, rice cakes/crackers). The clam component (60% of dish by weight) has negligible glycemic contribution (GI ~0 for shellfish). Weighted dish GI estimated at approximately 52 (0.60 × 0 + 0.25 × 82 + 0.15 × 15 for aromatics/oil), yielding a low-to-medium GI. Conservatively classified as low GI (≤55). GL per 300 g serving = (52 × 14.5 × 3) / 100 = 22.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 310 mg per 100 g; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 930 mg sodium, representing about 40% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request reduced seasoning, as the sodium from clam broth, soy-based sauces, and added salt in this dish can contribute substantially to daily sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice cracker component has a high individual GI (~82); estimated dish GI of ~52 is low-moderate, but carbohydrate load per full serving (~43.5 g) warrants portion control.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should moderate portion size to reduce glycemic load; the clam component provides high-quality protein that may blunt postprandial glucose response, but the rice cracker should be consumed in limited quantity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Clams are rich in omega-3 fatty acids and vitamin B12, which are cardioprotective, but sodium content and stir-frying oil add cardiovascular risk factors.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may benefit from the lean protein and micronutrient profile of baby clams, but should be mindful of the sodium content and request oil-reduced preparation to limit saturated and total fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Baby Clams with Rice Cracker, known in Vietnamese as Hến Xúc Bánh Đa (Hen Xuc Banh Da), is a Vietnamese-origin dish that has become popular in Singapore, particularly at Vietnamese-Singaporean restaurants such as Co Chung Restaurant. The dish features tender baby clams (hến) that are rapidly stir-fried at high heat with aromatic ingredients including garlic, onion, ginger, fresh chilli, and black pepper, then finished with fresh herbs. The clams are served alongside or scooped onto crispy rice crackers (bánh đa), which are large, thin, toasted or fried rice wafers that provide a satisfying crunch and act as an edible vessel. The flavour profile is savoury, slightly spicy, and umami-rich from the clams, with the rice cracker adding a neutral, crispy textural contrast. Nutritionally, baby clams are an excellent source of lean protein (approximately 11 g per 100 g of dish), exceptionally high in vitamin B12 (approximately 22 mcg per 100 g, far exceeding daily requirements), and a good source of iron (approximately 8.2 mg per 100 g), making this dish nutritionally dense. The dish is moderate in calories (~148 kcal per 100 g) and contains moderate sodium (~310 mg per 100 g), which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index of the dish is estimated at approximately 52 (low), driven primarily by the rice cracker component. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, portion control of the rice cracker is advisable. The dish is dairy-free and suitable for pescatarians. It is typically served as a main course or shared dish in a multi-course meal setting.

confidence_reason: No exact match found in HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA for this specific dish. Nutrient values estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data (cooked clams and rice crackers) using a weighted proportion model. GI estimated from analogous rice cracker GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the estimation approach; values are defensible but not derived from a direct dish-level measurement.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source data for this dish composition. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g, confirmed as negligible/trace in the USDA SR Legacy data for cooked clams and rice crackers; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central component data. The dish originates from Vietnamese cuisine (Hến Xúc Bánh Đa is a traditional Vietnamese dish from Hue/Central Vietnam) but is served in Singapore at Vietnamese restaurants and is classified under Singapore cuisine per the input source. Multilingual names include Vietnamese as the language of origin. The food_group has been reclassified from 'Grains and staples' (input) to 'Fish and seafood' as the primary protein component is baby clams; the rice cracker is an accompaniment. Halal status is marked false as clam dishes may not be universally certified halal in Singapore restaurant contexts; verification with the specific restaurant is recommended. Iron content is notably high due to the clam component, which is a well-established characteristic of bivalve shellfish.
